title: The Long-Game Forecast
subtitle: What ESG & climate risk is

hook.metaphor: Most risks are about this quarter or this year. ESG and climate risk are about the long-range forecast — the slow-moving changes in the environment, society, and how companies are governed that will reshape business over years and decades. Ignoring them feels safe today, the way ignoring a distant weather front feels safe — right up until it arrives, by which time it's too late to prepare.
hook.scenario: A coastal factory is highly profitable and well-run today. But sea levels are rising, a carbon tax is coming, and customers increasingly boycott high-polluters. None of this hurts this quarter's numbers. Should the board care now — or is this 'someone else's problem for later'?

learn: **ESG** stands for **Environmental, Social, and Governance** — three lenses on the non-financial factors that increasingly affect a company's long-term value and risk. **Climate risk** is the biggest piece of the 'E', and often discussed in its own right.

The defining feature is the **time horizon**: ESG and climate risks build slowly and land later — but they're often huge and partly irreversible when they do. This makes them uniquely easy to under-prioritise (our profitable factory) and uniquely dangerous to ignore, because by the time they hit your quarterly numbers, your options have narrowed.

Climate risk splits into two flavours you'll meet next level: **physical risk** (the direct damage of a changing climate — floods, fires, rising seas) and **transition risk** (the upheaval of *moving* to a low-carbon economy — carbon taxes, new rules, shifting demand). Our factory faces both.

ESG isn't charity or PR — it's increasingly **material financial risk**. A flooded factory, a stranded asset, a consumer boycott, or a governance scandal hits the balance sheet as hard as any credit or market loss. That's why risk professionals, investors, and regulators now treat it as core risk, not a feel-good extra.

And it connects to everything you've learned: climate change drives **operational** losses (damaged sites), **credit** losses (borrowers hit by climate), and **market** losses (repriced assets). ESG is less a separate silo than a long-horizon lens over all the other risks — which is exactly why regulators like Australia's **APRA** (via CPG 229) and global bodies now expect it to be managed.
bridge: So at work, you can resist the 'that's a problem for later' reflex by asking: *what slow-moving environmental, social, or governance shifts will reshape our business — and what do they cost us if we wait until they're obvious?* ESG and climate risk reward those who read the long-range forecast early, because by the time the storm front is overhead, the cheap options to prepare have already passed.

interactions.title: Care now, or later?
interactions.prompt: The profitable coastal factory faces rising seas, a coming carbon tax, and shifting consumer sentiment — none hurting this quarter. Should the board act now?

options.label: No — it doesn't affect current profits, so ignore it.
options.feedback: This is the classic ESG trap: slow-moving risks feel ignorable until they're suddenly upon you. By the time sea rise and carbon tax hit the numbers, the cheap options to adapt are gone.

options.label: Yes — these are material long-horizon risks; acting early is cheaper than reacting late.
options.feedback: Exactly. Physical (sea rise) and transition (carbon tax, boycotts) risks are real and partly irreversible. Reading the forecast early gives options that vanish once the storm arrives.

options.label: It's purely a PR/charity matter, not real risk.
options.feedback: A flooded factory, a carbon tax, and lost customers are financial losses, not PR. ESG here is material risk, not corporate goodwill.

interactions.title: Which letter of ESG?
interactions.prompt: Sort each issue by whether it's mainly Environmental, Social, or Governance.

items.label: How workers and communities are treated

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “ESG and climate risk are the long-range forecast — slow-moving environmental, social, and governance shifts that don't hit this quarter but land hard later — so they're material financial risk best prepared for early.”

checkpoint.explain: Three lenses on non-financial factors affecting long-term value and risk.

checkpoint.q: What makes ESG/climate risk uniquely easy to under-prioritise?
options: Its long time horizon — it builds slowly and lands later
checkpoint.explain: Slow-building risks feel ignorable until they arrive — by which point options have narrowed.

options: Material financial risk that connects to credit, market, and operational risk
checkpoint.explain: Floods, stranded assets, boycotts, and scandals hit the balance sheet like any other loss.
